title: Containers stuck in "stopping" after reboot
body:
### Issue Description

Very akin to this problem here: https://github.com/containers/podman/issues/11274
If I reboot (gracefully or ungraceful), the containers are left in a `Stopping` state.


### Steps to reproduce the issue

Steps to reproduce the issue
1. start a container
2. `sudo reboot`
3. any podman command regarding containers


### Describe the results you received

Any podman command, including trying to start up the container again yields the following error:

```
ERRO[0000] invalid internal status, try resetting the pause process with "podman system migrate": could not find any running process: no such process
```

Stopping any container yields the following errors:
```
ERRO[0000] getting rootless network namespace: unknown FS magic on "/tmp/podman-run-1000/netns/rootless-netns-24124f2bd7262c44004d": 58465342 
ERRO[0000] Unable to clean up network for container a6de1ed5324b9822dae4bcf91bf1442a708769c72f6a70aa7df899954bab601a: "unmounting network namespace for container a6de1ed5324b9822dae4bcf91bf1442a708769c72f6a70aa7df899954bab601a: failed to unmount NS: at /tmp/podman-run-1000/netns/netns-ce335784-ec12-44c8-cd6c-2a1526f95bbd: invalid argument" 
ERRO[0000] Free container lock: no such file or directory
```

Manually stopping the containers before the reboot works without issue.

### Describe the results you expected

The containers should start properly stopped.

### podman info output

```


### Podman in a container

No

### Privileged Or Rootless

Rootless

### Upstream Latest Release

Yes

### Additional environment details

void linux on Hetzner Cloud, 64 bit VM. the issues were not there on a Ubuntu installation and are not reproducible locally.

### Additional information

_No response_

comments.body: `/tmp/containers-user-1000/containers` is this on a tmpfs? If not the runroot directory must be removed after a reboot before you run any podman commands.
great catch. I indeed had /tmp persisted through a mount. Added a cronjob to clear /tmp on boot and will just revert to using a tmpfs in the future. Thanks!
